title: Alepidocline trifida
taxonomy: Alepidocline trifida is classified in the plant kingdom, specifically within the family Orobanchaceae, which is a group of flowering plants commonly known as broomrapes or holoparasites. This classification suggests that Alepidocline trifida is a type of parasitic plant that obtains its nutrients by connecting with the roots of other plants. Within the Orobanchaceae family, Alepidocline trifida is further classified within the genus Alepidocline, which is a small group of parasitic plants native to Central and South America. The family Orobanchaceae is part of the larger order Lamiales, which includes a wide range of flowering plants, including mint, snapdragons, and foxgloves. Overall, the classification of Alepidocline trifida provides insight into its evolutionary relationships with other plants.

intro: Alepidocline trifida, also known as Puebla snakeroot, is a perennial plant species in the Ageratum genus, native to Mexico and Central America. The plant has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties, offering potential health benefits such as anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial effects, which can aid in wound healing and reduce symptoms of various infections. Bioactive constituents of Alepidocline trifida include flavonoids, sesquiterpenes, and alkaloids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, Alepidocline trifida is often prepared as a tea or tincture, and is used to treat a range of conditions, including fever, sore throats, and respiratory issues.
